nativeRange.habitat: Tropical reef habitats with ample rockwork, ledges, caves, and crevices used for perching, grazing, and hiding.

decorPreferences: well-established live rock with ledges, caves, and crevices
mature reef tank with stable parameters

diet.notes: Has been reported as an opportunistic omnivore feeding on detritus, leftover food, small invertebrates, and Aiptasia anemones. Generally accepts a variety of meaty foods (mysis, brine, finely chopped seafood), pellets, and flake. Magnesium- and iodine-rich foods or supplementation are commonly recommended to support successful molting.

avoidWith: predatory fish that target shrimp (large hawkfish, lionfish, triggers, large wrasses, frogfish, anglerfish)
small ornamental polyps if shrimp are underfed

summary: A rarely-imported Indo-Pacific Lysmata that bridges cleaner and peppermint shrimp behavior: scarlet-cleaner sized with peppermint-style striping. Generally considered reef-safe with caution and is sometimes used for Aiptasia control.
careNotes: Specialty Indo-Pacific cleaner shrimp occasionally available through importers like Aqua Imports, Pacific East Aquaculture, and Quality Marine; supply is sporadic. Husbandry parallels that of other Lysmata cleaners. Generally advised to add only to well-established, parameter-stable reef systems with abundant live rock providing ledges, caves, and crevices for perching and hiding. Multiples are typically tolerated and have been reported to live socially in small groups, which is unusual relative to some other Lysmata. Has been reported to opportunistically consume Aiptasia anemones, though effectiveness varies between individual shrimp. Generally considered reef-safe with caution: well-fed specimens tend to ignore corals, but underfed individuals have been reported to nip small polyps or steal food intended for corals. Like all crustaceans this species is highly sensitive to copper-based medications and to abrupt salinity or temperature swings — drip-acclimate carefully and never dose copper into the display. Magnesium and iodine are commonly cited as important for molting. Cross-source notes: maximum temperatures range from 78°F (LiveAquaria, Quality Marine, Aqua Imports) to 80°F (Pacific East); pH 8.1–8.4 consistent across sources; salinity stated as 1.023–1.025 SG (LiveAquaria, Quality Marine, Pacific East) up to 35 ppt / ~1.026 SG (Aqua Imports); dKH 8–12 (LiveAquaria, Quality Marine) vs 8–9 (Pacific East). Averaged values used above. Adult size is most often stated at 1.5–2 inches (~4–5 cm), with some hobbyist accounts of individuals reaching ~3 inches; written here as 3–5 cm. No source explicitly states a minimum tank volume; 75 L (~20 gal) is used as a sensible floor for a mature reef capable of supporting a small group, biased to a nice round value per project convention.
breedingNotes: Has been bred in captivity by hobbyists and small-scale aquaculturists (e.g., aquaculture breeding journals on TCMAS), but is not commercially aquacultured at scale. Like other Lysmata, this species is reported to be a protandric simultaneous hermaphrodite: individuals first mature as functional males and later develop functional female reproductive organs while retaining male function, so any two adults can potentially form a breeding pair. Larvae are planktonic and require dedicated rearing tanks, microalgae and rotifer/Artemia nauplii feeds, and careful water management — generally regarded as a difficult target for home breeders.

primary.name: Aqua Imports
primary.notes: Primary source for origin (Indo-Pacific) and target water parameters (pH 8.2–8.4, 74–78°F, salinity 35 ppt ≈ SG 1.026). Product page text was thin; deeper husbandry filled in from secondaries.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Designated secondary did not return a species-specific page on access; swapped to LiveAquaria, Pacific East Aquaculture, and Quality Marine as confirming secondaries. URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: LiveAquaria
additional.notes: Secondary substitute. Confirms family Hippolytidae (legacy; current placement Lysmatidae), max size ~2", temperament peaceful, reef-safe with caution, temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.023–1.025, omnivore feeding on Aiptasia and detritus, care level easy.

additional.name: Pacific East Aquaculture
additional.notes: Secondary. Confirms ~1.5" adult size, temperature 76–80°F, SG ~1.025, dKH 8–9, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, reef-safe, useful for Aiptasia control, can be kept in small groups.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Secondary. Confirms ~2" adult size, peaceful and social (multiples acceptable), reef-safe, temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, beginner care level, copper-medication intolerance, importance of magnesium and iodine for molting.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally molts on the order of every few weeks as the shrimp grows, with discarded exoskeletons commonly left in the tank and re-consumed for calcium. Magnesium and iodine are widely cited as important for successful molts; chronically low minerals or unstable salinity have been associated with incomplete-molt mortality. Drip-acclimation and stable parameters are commonly considered the single most important factor in molting success.
crustacean.breedingNotes: See top-level breedingNotes — larval rearing is the bottleneck for this Lysmata, as for most cleaner shrimp.
